name: Why Allah tells us their stories
epithet: An introduction to the prophets, peace be upon them all
title: Why Allah tells us the stories of the prophets: an introduction
meta_description: Day 1 of 29: before the first prophet, why Allah fills the Qur'an with their stories, the difference between a messenger and a prophet, and the one quality that makes them His chosen. Retold faithfully from Mufti Ismail Menk's Stories of the Prophets.
keywords: stories of the prophets, Mufti Menk, qasas al-anbiya, why study the prophets, rasool and nabi, prophets in the Quran, Adam to Isa, Buruja

video.title: Stories Of The Prophets-01~Introduction To Stories Of The Prophets

source_note: Retold faithfully from Mufti Ismail Menk's Stories of the Prophets, episode 1: the introduction (delivered on the first night of Ramadan 1432, in Cape Town). Qur'an: Sahih International, verified via quran.ai. The narration is Mufti Menk's, the phrasing is Buruja's.
lede: Before a single prophet is named, before the clay of Adam or the ark of Nuh or the staff of Musa, there is a question worth a whole evening: why did Allah fill so much of the Qur'an with their stories at all? He is not a storyteller passing time. So what are these accounts of drowned nations and tested fathers and broken idols actually for?

This is day one of twenty-nine, a walk through the prophets who came before the final one ﷺ, retold faithfully from Mufti Ismail Menk's beloved series. And like any good teacher, he begins not with the first story, but with why there are stories.

sections.h2: Allah did not leave us guessing

ayah.arabic: لَقَدْ كَانَ فِى قَصَصِهِمْ عِبْرَةٌ لِّأُو۟لِى ٱلْأَلْبَٰبِ ۗ مَا كَانَ حَدِيثًا يُفْتَرَىٰ وَلَٰكِن تَصْدِيقَ ٱلَّذِى بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ وَتَفْصِيلَ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ وَهُدًى وَرَحْمَةً لِّقَوْمٍ يُؤْمِنُونَ
ayah.text: There was certainly in their stories a lesson for those of understanding. Never was it [i.e., the Qur’ān] a narration invented, but a confirmation of what was before it and a detailed explanation of all things and guidance and mercy for a people who believe.

paras: Allah made you, and He did not then walk away. He is ar-Rabb, a word Mufti Menk pauses on because no single English word holds it: the One who created you, nourishes you, provides for you, protects you, and holds every atom of your existence in His hand. A Lord like that does not create a servant and leave him to guess his way home. He sent a manual, and He sent men to carry it. There is no nation that has passed, not one, without a warner sent to it.

So the stories of the prophets are not folklore, and they are not bedtime tales we dress in robes. Allah Himself tells us what they are: a lesson for people of understanding, not invented, but a confirmation of the truth and a detailed explanation and a guidance and a mercy. That is the whole reason we will spend twenty-nine days here. Every one of these lives was preserved by Allah on purpose, for you.

sections.h2: A messenger, a prophet, and you

ayah.arabic: وَوَهَبْنَا لَهُۥ مِن رَّحْمَتِنَآ أَخَاهُ هَٰرُونَ نَبِيًّا
ayah.text: And We gave him out of Our mercy his brother Aaron as a prophet.

paras: Mufti Menk draws a line the Qur'an itself draws. A rasool, a messenger, is sent with something new: a fresh law, a book, a command from Allah to deliver. A nabi, a prophet, carries the message of a messenger who came before him. Every messenger is a prophet, but not every prophet is a messenger. When Allah gave Musa, peace be upon him, the message, He gave him his brother Harun alongside him as a prophet to help him. The message was Musa's to bring; Harun was sent to hold him up.

And then comes the line that should make you sit up, because it is about you. There is a third kind of carrier, neither messenger nor prophet, and it is every single one of us. We are the messengers of the Messenger ﷺ. He carried Allah's final message, and then he turned to us and said: convey from me, even if it is one verse. So you are not a spectator at these stories. You are the next link in the chain that runs from Allah, to His prophets, to His final Prophet ﷺ, and now to you, with a duty to learn it, live it, and pass it on.

sections.h2: Why we read it here, and nowhere else
paras: How man began, why he is here, what happened to the nations that vanished: none of this can be reached by digging or guessing. No scientist can hand you the name of the first human being or tell you what was said to him. It can only come from the One who was there, through revelation. That is what makes a believer a believer.

Open the older scriptures and you will find the same prophets, the same broad outlines, because they began from the same source. But Mufti Menk is honest about why we do not lean on them: their keepers are still arguing among themselves over which version is authentic, and too much has crept in and been altered. So we take our account from the Qur'an, which Allah Himself has kept pure, the one book no hand has been allowed to change. When the Qur'an tells the story of Nuh or Yusuf or Musa, peace be upon them, you are not reading a remembered legend. You are reading the record of the One who watched it happen. This is why these stories are the natural doorway back into the Qur'an itself, the book your whole faith is built on.

sections.h2: A book to be lived in, not leaned on

ayah.arabic: كِتَٰبٌ أَنزَلْنَٰهُ إِلَيْكَ مُبَٰرَكٌ لِّيَدَّبَّرُوٓا۟ ءَايَٰتِهِۦ وَلِيَتَذَكَّرَ أُو۟لُوا۟ ٱلْأَلْبَٰبِ
ayah.text: [This is] a blessed Book which We have revealed to you, [O Muḥammad], that they might reflect upon its verses and that those of understanding would be reminded.

paras: Why did Allah reveal the Qur'an? He answers the question Himself: so that its verses would be pondered. Not recited and shelved like a pillow under the head at night, but understood. Mufti Menk asks it plainly, and it stings a little: how many of us have read a shelf of novels cover to cover, and could not say what the verse we recited this morning actually meant? He gently presses everyone, himself included, to read a portion of the Qur'an in a language they understand, because a manual you cannot read cannot run your life.

And here is the mercy in where we are starting. The stories of the prophets make up the great bulk of the Qur'an, and they are the most accessible part of it: anyone can follow a story. So this is not a detour away from the Qur'an. It is the easiest road straight into it. Walk these twenty-nine days and you will find you are no longer a stranger to large stretches of the book.

sections.h2: The kind of person Allah chooses

ayah.arabic: وَمَا يَنطِقُ عَنِ ٱلْهَوَىٰٓ إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا وَحْىٌ يُوحَىٰ
ayah.text: Nor does he speak from [his own] inclination. It is not but a revelation revealed,

paras: Allah never picked the mediocre to carry His word. Mufti Menk walks through the qualities every prophet shared, and as he does, notice that you are watching a portrait of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ take shape, the one whose life the whole of this chain is climbing toward. They were truthful, every one, known for it by their own people long before any revelation came, the way Makkah called him ﷺ the truthful and the trustworthy before they knew what he would become. They were highly intelligent: Ibrahim, peace be upon him, debated more than any of them, questioning idols and kings while still a young man. They were people you were drawn to, not repelled by; of the Prophet ﷺ a companion said simply, when I saw his face I knew this is not the face of a liar.

Allah trained them first with flocks, for there was no prophet who had not at some point herded sheep, learning patience on animals before he was trusted with people. He protected them from sin. And what they spoke was not their own opinion: of His final Messenger ﷺ Allah says he does not speak from desire, it is only revelation. None of them ever asked for a wage. Follow, the Qur'an says, those who ask of you no reward and are themselves rightly guided. When you meet Nuh and Ibrahim and Musa in the coming days, you already know the family they belong to.

sections.h2: Why the stories are really told

ayah.arabic: أَمْ حَسِبْتُمْ أَن تَدْخُلُوا۟ ٱلْجَنَّةَ وَلَمَّا يَأْتِكُم مَّثَلُ ٱلَّذِينَ خَلَوْا۟ مِن قَبْلِكُم ۖ مَّسَّتْهُمُ ٱلْبَأْسَآءُ وَٱلضَّرَّآءُ وَزُلْزِلُوا۟ حَتَّىٰ يَقُولَ ٱلرَّسُولُ وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ مَعَهُۥ مَتَىٰ نَصْرُ ٱللَّهِ ۗ أَلَآ إِنَّ نَصْرَ ٱللَّهِ قَرِيبٌ
ayah.text: Or do you think that you will enter Paradise while such [trial] has not yet come to you as came to those who passed on before you? They were touched by poverty and hardship and were shaken until [even their] messenger and those who believed with him said, "When is the help of Allāh?" Unquestionably, the help of Allāh is near.

paras: Here is the lesson Mufti Menk says runs underneath all of them. The people of Nuh, peace be upon him, transgressed for a very long time, and to the eye of the moment it looked like wrong was winning and right was drowning. Then the flood came, and it was the other way around. So when you watch evil look like it is winning, the prophets' stories tell you the ending in advance: the people of goodness win. Maybe not immediately. But ultimately, always.

And there is a warning folded inside that comfort: none of it comes without a test. Did you think you would enter Paradise untouched, when those before you were shaken with poverty and hardship until even the messenger cried out, when is the help of Allah? Mufti Menk gives the image of a school. The higher the grade, the harder the exam, and the real life is not the years inside the school but everything after you graduate. The prophets sat the hardest exams ever set, and Allah preserved their papers so we would know how the test is passed. Tomorrow, the first paper: the creation of the first human being.

dua.en: My Lord, increase me in knowledge. (Surah Ta-Ha 20:114)

lessons_intro: Before a single prophet is named, the introduction hands you a way to read all of them. These threads run straight out of Mufti Menk's opening night.

lessons.bold: Read them as letters to you.
lessons.text: Allah preserved these lives as a lesson for people of understanding, not as history. Ask of every story, not what happened, but what it is asking of me.
Messenger, prophet, and then you, the one who carries the message onward. Convey from me even one verse, he ﷺ said. These stories are yours to learn and to pass on.

lessons.bold: Let the stories walk you into the Qur'an.
lessons.text: They are the bulk of the book and the easiest part to follow. Read a little in a language you understand, and the Qur'an stops being a stranger.
No one entered Paradise untouched, not even the prophets. When hardship comes, it is the exam, not a sign Allah has forgotten you. His help is near.
Evil can look like it is winning for a long time. The prophets' stories give away the ending: goodness wins, maybe not at once, but always in the end.

faq.q: What is the difference between a rasool and a nabi?
faq.a: A rasool (messenger) is sent with something new from Allah: a fresh law, a book, a command to deliver. A nabi (prophet) carries and upholds the message of a messenger who came before him. Every messenger is a prophet, but not every prophet is a messenger. The Qur'an gives the example of Musa, who was given the message, and his brother Harun, who was sent alongside him as a prophet to help him (Surah Maryam 19:53).

faq.q: Why study the prophets from the Qur'an rather than older scriptures?
faq.a: The same prophets appear in the older scriptures because they share one origin, but Mufti Menk notes that their keepers still dispute which versions are authentic, and much has been altered over time. The Qur'an is the one account Allah has preserved unchanged, so it is the account we rely on. How the first human being was created, and why, can only be known through revelation in the first place.

faq.q: Where in the Qur'an does Allah say why He tells these stories?
faq.a: In Surah Yusuf 12:111: 'There was certainly in their stories a lesson for those of understanding. Never was it a narration invented, but a confirmation of what was before it and a detailed explanation of all things and guidance and mercy for a people who believe.' Allah states plainly that the stories are a lesson, not entertainment.

faq.q: How does this connect to the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ?
faq.a: Every quality of the earlier prophets, truthfulness, trustworthiness, intelligence, being protected from sin, speaking only by revelation, is fulfilled most completely in the final Prophet ﷺ. The whole chain of prophets climbs toward him, which is why this series leads naturally into the Seerah, the life of the Prophet ﷺ.

faq.q: Is belief in the prophets required of a Muslim?
faq.a: Yes. Belief in all of Allah's prophets and messengers is one of the six articles of faith (iman). A Muslim believes in them all without distinction, from Adam to Isa, and in Muhammad ﷺ as the last of them. Learning their stories is how that belief becomes real rather than a name on a list.

closing_heading: Why this day stays with you
closing: Twenty-nine days from now you will have walked from the first human being shaped by Allah's hand to Isa, peace be upon him, the prophet who pointed ahead to a messenger named Ahmad ﷺ, with the whole Qur'an opening up around you as you go. Day one exists so you walk it the right way: not as a museum of old nations, but as a series of letters Allah wrote and kept, each one addressed to you, each one a lesson, a warning, and a mercy.

So begin the way the prophets themselves began, low before your Lord and hungry to understand. O Allah, increase us in knowledge that benefits us, let these stories of Your prophets soften our hearts and straighten our steps, join us to the chain that carries Your message, and gather us with Your prophets and Your final Messenger ﷺ in the home You prepared for those who believe. Ameen.

Before the first prophet is named, one question worth a whole evening: why did Allah fill the Qur'an with their stories at all?

He is not a storyteller passing time.

Allah says their stories are a lesson for people of understanding. Not invented. A guidance and a mercy.

Every one was preserved on purpose. For you.

Messenger. Prophet. And you.

A rasool is sent with something new. A nabi upholds a message already sent.

And the third carrier? Every one of us. The messengers of the Messenger ﷺ.

Convey from me, he ﷺ said, even one verse.

You are not a spectator at these stories.

You are the next link in the chain.

Why read it here and nowhere else?

No scientist can name the first human being. It can only come by revelation.

The older scriptures were altered. The Qur'an, Allah kept pure.

The kind of person Allah chooses:

Truthful. Trustworthy. Drawn to, not repelled by. Protected from sin. Speaking only by revelation. Never asking a wage.

A portrait of the Prophet ﷺ, taking shape.

The lesson under all of them:

When evil looks like it is winning, the prophets' stories give away the ending.

Goodness wins. Maybe not immediately. But ultimately, always.

Did you think you would enter Paradise untouched?

The prophets sat the hardest exams ever set. Allah kept their papers so we would know how the test is passed.

social.caption: Day 1 of 29: the Stories of the Prophets begin. Before Adam, before Nuh, before Musa, one question: why does Allah tell us their stories at all? Because they are a lesson, a guidance, and a mercy, and because the whole chain leads to him ﷺ. Retold faithfully from Mufti Ismail Menk's beloved series (the episode is embedded on the site), Qur'an verified via quran.ai. A prophet a day, every day, connected back to the Qur'an and the life of the Prophet ﷺ, at buruja.com. #StoriesOfTheProphets #MuftiMenk #prophets #Quran #Buruja #islam #seerah #iman

email.subject: Why does Allah tell us their stories?
email.alt_subject: Before the first prophet, one question
email.preview: Day 1 of 29: the Stories of the Prophets begin, and not with a story.
email.body:
Assalamu alaykum,

Today Buruja begins a new walk: twenty-nine days through the lives of the prophets who came before the final one ﷺ, retold faithfully from Mufti Ismail Menk's beloved Stories of the Prophets.

And it does not begin with a story. It begins with a question worth a whole evening: why did Allah fill so much of the Qur'an with these accounts of drowned nations and tested fathers and broken idols at all?

Because He did not leave us guessing. Allah made us, and then He sent a manual and men to carry it, a warner to every nation that ever lived. And He tells us plainly what their stories are for: 'There was certainly in their stories a lesson for those of understanding... a guidance and mercy for a people who believe' (Surah Yusuf 12:111). Not folklore. Letters, written and preserved by Allah, each one addressed to you.

Today's entry walks through the introduction: the difference between a messenger and a prophet, and the surprising third kind of carrier, which is you, the messenger of the Messenger ﷺ. Why we take these stories from the Qur'an and nowhere else. The qualities that made these men Allah's chosen, every one of them a portrait of the Prophet ﷺ whose life this chain is climbing toward. And the lesson underneath all of them: that goodness wins in the end, but never without the test.

Tomorrow, the first human being. Tonight, just one du'a, the one Allah taught: My Lord, increase me in knowledge.

Read day 1, and watch Mufti Menk's full opening, at the link below.
